fullDescription.text: The application uses http.createServer() instead of https.createServer(), transmitting all data including session cookies and credentials in cleartext. The HTTPS configuration code exists but is entirely commented out.

shortDescription.text: Private key committed to source repository

fullDescription.text: A TLS private key (server.key) is committed to the source code repository under artifacts/cert/. This exposes the key to all repository users and compromises any TLS configuration that uses it.

Self-signed certificates in artifacts/cert/ cannot be validated by clients against a trusted certificate authority. Even if HTTPS were enabled, clients would need to bypass certificate validation, defeating the purpose of TLS.

shortDescription.text: HTTP Strict Transport Security not enabled

fullDescription.text: The helmet.hsts() middleware call is commented out. Without HSTS headers, browsers will not enforce HTTPS connections, leaving users vulnerable to protocol downgrade and man-in-the-middle attacks.

shortDescription.text: No TLS cipher suite or protocol version configuration

fullDescription.text: The commented-out HTTPS options specify only key and cert without ciphers, secureProtocol, or minVersion. If enabled as-is, the server would accept weak cipher suites and older TLS versions including TLS 1.0/1.1.

message.text: Application uses http.createServer() on line 288 instead of https.createServer(). All traffic including authentication credentials, session tokens, and user data is transmitted in cleartext, enabling eavesdropping and man-in-the-middle attacks. The HTTPS fix code (lines 293-297) is commented out.

TLS private key file (server.key) is committed to the source repository at artifacts/cert/server.key. Any user with repository access can extract this key and impersonate the server or decrypt captured TLS traffic. Private keys must never be stored in version control.

The HTTPS server configuration (lines 161-171) is entirely commented out. The code references fs.readFileSync for loading server.key and server.crt but is non-functional, meaning TLS is never established regardless of certificate presence.

Self-signed TLS certificate (server.crt) stored in artifacts/cert/. Self-signed certificates cannot be validated against a trusted CA chain, making it impossible for clients to verify server identity. This facilitates man-in-the-middle attacks even if HTTPS is enabled.
HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS) via helmet.hsts() is commented out on line 197. Without HSTS, browsers will not enforce secure connections, allowing protocol downgrade attacks such as SSLStrip.

The commented-out httpsOptions (lines 167-170) specifies only key and cert without configuring ciphers, secureProtocol, or minVersion. If HTTPS were enabled as-is, the server would negotiate with default Node.js settings which may include weak cipher suites (RC4, DES) and deprecated protocol versions (TLS 1.0, TLS 1.1).
